CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): Overall the paper is reasonably organized (Intro → Related Work → Methods → Experiments → Conclusion) and the core methods (brute force, 1st/2nd-order Taylor, two algorithms) are presented clearly in Sections 3–3.3. However presentation problems hamper readability: numerous formatting/editing errors (e.g. repeated paragraphs about quantization, a “Figure ??” placeholder, duplicated text blocks), occasional awkward phrasing and run‑on sentences, and missing/unclear figure references make some experimental descriptions hard to follow. Notation is generally consistent for the key derivatives and ∆Ek expressions, but the paper defers many derivations to the Appendix and sometimes glosses over how second‑order terms are computed, which reduces immediate clarity. | B(3): Overall the paper follows a clear high-level organization (Introduction → Literature Review → Methods → Experiments → Conclusion) and states its aims early. However presentation problems reduce clarity: there are repeated and mangled paragraphs (multiple verbatim repetitions about weight quantization and pruning), placeholder/missing figure references (e.g. "Figure ??"), and some abrupt asides that interrupt the flow. Notation (E, O_k, ΔE_k and Taylor approximations) is reasonably consistent, but several derivations are deferred to an appendix and some symbols/assumptions are not fully explained in-text. The writing is generally understandable but contains occasional grammatical awkwardness and verbosity that make parts of the experimental description and visualization discussion harder to follow.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is reasonably organized (Intro → Related Work → Methods → Experiments → Conclusion) and the core methods (brute force, 1st/2nd-order Taylor, two algorithms) are presented clearly in Sections 3–3.3. However presentation problems hamper readability: numerous formatting/editing errors (e.g. repeated paragraphs about quantization, a “Figure ??” placeholder, duplicated text blocks), occasional awkward phrasing and run‑on sentences, and missing/unclear figure references make some experimental descriptions hard to follow. Notation is generally consistent for the key derivatives and ∆Ek expressions, but the paper defers many derivations to the Appendix and sometimes glosses over how second‑order terms are computed, which reduces immediate clarity.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper follows a clear high-level organization (Introduction → Literature Review → Methods → Experiments → Conclusion) and states its aims early. However presentation problems reduce clarity: there are repeated and mangled paragraphs (multiple verbatim repetitions about weight quantization and pruning), placeholder/missing figure references (e.g. "Figure ??"), and some abrupt asides that interrupt the flow. Notation (E, O_k, ΔE_k and Taylor approximations) is reasonably consistent, but several derivations are deferred to an appendix and some symbols/assumptions are not fully explained in-text. The writing is generally understandable but contains occasional grammatical awkwardness and verbosity that make parts of the experimental description and visualization discussion harder to follow.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a concrete algorithmic variant — pruning whole neurons via a second-order Taylor-series approximation and two removal schedules (single overall ranking and iterative re-ranking) (Sections 3.2–3.3) — and uses extensive empirical comparison to first-order and brute-force baselines. However the work explicitly builds on and adapts classic pruning ideas (Skeletonization, Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon) and admits it ‘‘borrows much of our inspiration’’ and makes similar diagonal-Hessian and serial-removal assumptions (Related work and Conclusions). Thus the contribution is a non-trivial, practically-oriented extension and empirical investigation rather than a fundamentally new theory or paradigm, i.e., moderate/ incremental novelty. | B(3): The paper provides a moderate, incremental novelty: it adapts classical sensitivity-based pruning (e.g., Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon, Skeletonization) to the unit level with a second-order Taylor approximation and an iterative re-ranking procedure (Sec. 3.2–3.3, Algorithms 1–2). Its empirical emphasis — exhaustive brute-force neuron-removal baseline and analysis corroborating Mozer & Smolensky’s ‘‘dualist’’ neuron-role observation — offers a useful perspective but is not a fundamentally new problem or theoretical framework. The work clearly cites and builds on prior methods (LeCun et al., Hassibi & Stork, Mozer & Smolensky) and mostly combines known techniques with a focused application and empirical study rather than introducing a highly novel algorithmic breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a concrete algorithmic variant — pruning whole neurons via a second-order Taylor-series approximation and two removal schedules (single overall ranking and iterative re-ranking) (Sections 3.2–3.3) — and uses extensive empirical comparison to first-order and brute-force baselines. However the work explicitly builds on and adapts classic pruning ideas (Skeletonization, Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon) and admits it ‘‘borrows much of our inspiration’’ and makes similar diagonal-Hessian and serial-removal assumptions (Related work and Conclusions). Thus the contribution is a non-trivial, practically-oriented extension and empirical investigation rather than a fundamentally new theory or paradigm, i.e., moderate/ incremental novelty.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a moderate, incremental novelty: it adapts classical sensitivity-based pruning (e.g., Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon, Skeletonization) to the unit level with a second-order Taylor approximation and an iterative re-ranking procedure (Sec. 3.2–3.3, Algorithms 1–2). Its empirical emphasis — exhaustive brute-force neuron-removal baseline and analysis corroborating Mozer & Smolensky’s ‘‘dualist’’ neuron-role observation — offers a useful perspective but is not a fundamentally new problem or theoretical framework. The work clearly cites and builds on prior methods (LeCun et al., Hassibi & Stork, Mozer & Smolensky) and mostly combines known techniques with a focused application and empirical study rather than introducing a highly novel algorithmic breakthrough.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper states clear claims and presents experiments (brute-force, 1st‑order and 2nd‑order Taylor pruning on toy problems and a MNIST subset) that qualitatively support those claims, but key mathematical derivations are deferred to an appendix not provided so correctness of the claimed 2nd‑order formulas and their implementation cannot be verified from the text. The experimental methodology is limited: use of a small/altered 5k MNIST subset, single runs, no statistical measures or multiple seeds, and no computational-cost analysis for the brute‑force baseline weaken generality of the results. The authors acknowledge and rely on strong assumptions (diagonal Hessian, serial greedy pruning) yet nevertheless generalize to broad conclusions (40–70% pruning feasible) that are only modestly supported by the presented, narrowly scoped experiments. | B(3): Main claims are stated and are supported by experiments that show brute‑force>2nd‑order>1st‑order in the reported settings, but the evidence is limited. The paper refers to nontrivial derivations placed in a Supplementary Appendix (not present here) so mathematical completeness and correctness cannot be verified; the single‑variable Taylor expansion and use of diagonal Hessian terms are plausible but only approximations and are acknowledged by the authors. The experimental methodology is under‑specified (MNIST reduced to 5k/20×20, few details on training, no multiple runs or statistical tests, limited baselines), so results may not generalize. Logical gaps include the diagonal‑Hessian and serial‑pruning assumptions and lack of complexity/robustness analysis; therefore the conclusions are partially but not fully justified by the presented material.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and presents experiments (brute-force, 1st‑order and 2nd‑order Taylor pruning on toy problems and a MNIST subset) that qualitatively support those claims, but key mathematical derivations are deferred to an appendix not provided so correctness of the claimed 2nd‑order formulas and their implementation cannot be verified from the text. The experimental methodology is limited: use of a small/altered 5k MNIST subset, single runs, no statistical measures or multiple seeds, and no computational-cost analysis for the brute‑force baseline weaken generality of the results. The authors acknowledge and rely on strong assumptions (diagonal Hessian, serial greedy pruning) yet nevertheless generalize to broad conclusions (40–70% pruning feasible) that are only modestly supported by the presented, narrowly scoped experiments.

reviewer_b.justification: Main claims are stated and are supported by experiments that show brute‑force>2nd‑order>1st‑order in the reported settings, but the evidence is limited. The paper refers to nontrivial derivations placed in a Supplementary Appendix (not present here) so mathematical completeness and correctness cannot be verified; the single‑variable Taylor expansion and use of diagonal Hessian terms are plausible but only approximations and are acknowledged by the authors. The experimental methodology is under‑specified (MNIST reduced to 5k/20×20, few details on training, no multiple runs or statistical tests, limited baselines), so results may not generalize. Logical gaps include the diagonal‑Hessian and serial‑pruning assumptions and lack of complexity/robustness analysis; therefore the conclusions are partially but not fully justified by the presented material.
